item.title: Other Landing Event destinations?
item.content:encoded: Would love to see other xenoscapes "found" when #2063 got placed on assorted items around the containment chamber. (For instance — Arcade game token, movie poster with fictional-but-non-SF setting, unadorned body pillow.)

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I couldn't find the source of the original image, so I've created a new one.

Source assets attribution
Source assets attribution

USS Enterprise model by TimothyDennisOrtega (CC-BY)
Peppermint Powerplant HDRI map by Andreas Mischok (CC0)
Concrete Tunnel 02 HDRI map by Andreas Mischok (CC0)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best thing that the random scp button has ever taken me to

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the images are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. I was considering doing some form of Trek homage skip at some future point, but I don't think this can ever be topped.

+1

item.title: Awesome.
item.content:encoded: This is one of my favorites, now I just want to read about all the different Landing Events.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Room connected to Earth's actual moon. D-class were immediately sucked out of the room by explosive decompression.

Correction, sir, that's "blown out".

(But actually I don't care which one you use, I just wanted to make the reference)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a minute…

SCP-2063

From the Wikipedia page about the year 2063 AD:

In Star Trek chronology, the starship USS Enterprise accidentally travels a little over 300 years into the past to April 4, 2063, while in pursuit of the Borg Queen spaceship. The crew of the Enterprise visit the US state of Montana on Earth to witness the historical events of the next day on April 5.

Magnificent bastard. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you do write a story for this one please let me know. I really want to read it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the test with the pepperoni pizza! 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you have any ideas for inexpensive/researcher-donated crap to land the ship on, let me know! I'll write it up!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey all. Been working my way up to a proper Tale based on this. After much thought, added the Teleportation and Toy tags. Resisted the urge to add Vehicle. Probably going to update containment procedures soon because the more I flesh out "what happened next" after the Battery Cover Test, the more I realize there's some serious holes in the Containment Procedures that would probably be patched long before it got to this point.

Stay tuned, and sorry for letting this lie fallow for so long.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Working on it! Check out my Sandbox page for the WIP version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're going to blatantly reference pop culture, you really have to capture the feel of the source material, not just the form. This one manages that, so +1.

Also, I gave the +1 when I encountered the red shirts joke, and didn't think the final note was much good - not that it was enough to affect the upvote, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did you ever end up writing/linking Document 2063-LE-L?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS is how to make a pop-culture referencing skip!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a Trekkie (or Trekker, or fan, whatever, I never know which is which), I of course love this.

The "cultural icon as SCP" is often attempted, but end up either being some thinly veiled fanfic crossover or some horrible grimdark parody. This one manages to be a good SCP on its own with the Star Trek references (discovering strange and new worlds, killing redshirts) subtle enough to be enjoyable without stealing the spotlight.

I however missed the point on the last note and needed to read the discussion - and even then I find it more evocative of the Last Starfighter than Trek. All in all I don't like it.

But upvote anyway because Star Trek.

item.title: Re: Ending
item.content:encoded: Yes, but the Foundation didn't build the ship. Someone else did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aside from the Star Trek references, I just think it's almost whimsical having an SCP that's a little spaceship flying around a room. I also liked how it was tested with pizza.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should be noted that D-class personnel tend to have a high mortality rate duing Landing Events. For reasons that are not well-understood (but which have been heavily speculated upon by researchers,) D-class personnel are invariably the first to be terminated by dangers present.

Slap red shirts on them and suddenly Star Trek.

I love this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually a version of the Holodeck, the Rec Room, first appeared in the Star Tried Animated Series episode "The Practical Joker." While technically not original series, it is original Enterprise.

item.title: Ending
item.content:encoded: So, does the note at the end imply that the Foundation is operating a secret space ship somewhere, utilizing SCP-2063 as the entrance? And that only particular personnel are chosen, while the rest of the Foundation has no idea of its existence?

item.title: I'm an inverse
item.content:encoded: While many people who read this didn't like the D-Class redshirt joke, that joke was actually my favorite part of SCP-2063.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, now I just want to put some of SCP-2558-J in it and see what happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am quite disappointed that it's not a Phoenix model. I want my money back! :I

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So all the Foundation has to do is harness the power of the deflector dish and no problem will be beyond them?

Or is that from TNG…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've already made it subtler a couple of times.

Jesus, now I feel stupid — I didn't get the redshirts joke until I checked out the discussion page.

Still, I liked it. And I liked the ending, too. A fun SCP with an optimistic ending, and it was enjoyable the whole way through. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I was just attempting a half-considered wordplay between Anton Chekhov, playwright, and Pavel Chekov, NCC-1701 helmsman (since this is a Trek themed SCP). Admittedly, when a joke comes across as a request for information, the joke has gone very, very badly! :)
Although it is always good to spread the word of Chekhov's shotgun!1
Well, you'll have to excuse me, I've got a malformed pun to put out of it's misery.

Footnotes
1. …actually, there are probably inappropriate times, like at a funeral, or when being held at gunpoint…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very briefly:

Chekov was a famous playwright who once said that (paraphrased) if you place a gun on the mantlepiece in the first act, it has to go off in the third. Essentially, if you deliberately introduce a dramatic element, you have to use it before the end of the story, preferably at the height of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it's not obvious enough, nobody picks up on it, and you're left with Chekov's guns your audience can't identify.

I don't know what it is at the moment, but a joke about Pavel's shotgun needs to be made.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, we're past the editing deadline, but thanks for the suggestions.

Once the contest is over, I plan to migrate over the testing logs as a separate document, link to it from the skip, and begin working more seriously on Tales.

Camera packages are a good idea. The flags are basically a low-cost precursor to that experiment. I'll have to add that.

Interesting thought about warping real locations to conform to the objects. But, no, there's an obvious distinction between using the skip for Simulated Travel and using the skip for Actual Travel. You know when you've crossed that line when stuff doesn't fade into nothingness following the termination of a Landing Event. Simulated Travel is simulated. There would be no reason for the stuff you bring back to dissolve, otherwise.

(Yes, the Holodeck is a TNG concept, but I'm using it anyway.)

You have to be very careful when making a skip that manipulates researchers assigned to it. If it's too obvious, you start to wonder why the Foundation is researching it at all, instead of just flying it around the room every 70 days. Is there no oversight? Are they just stupid? If it's not obvious enough, nobody picks up on it, and you're left with Chekov's guns your audience can't identify.

Anyway, I wanted a skip that bears repeat trials, like the vending machines, and rewards creative test ideas with novel results, like the Refinery. Not something they played with for a month and then locked away because it was too dangerous to mess with.

The note at the end explicitly states that some of the destinations are Real. It very strongly hints that one of these real destinations is in fact an actual Starship.

Later Consequences of this discovery are the reason the note at the end exists. One does not simply walk away from an alien Starship after one has had access to the Bridge. It's too late for that. Now that we know it's Real, the Starship must be Contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. I love this!!!
Just a couple thoughts that might make the tone of the entry be more "SCP" while still being all Trek.1
First, it would be easy to remove the images and black the name while still giving enough to make the subject and tone unambiguous. It would be less gratuitous than a lot of Foundation redaction.
Second, while I adore the note at the end, when you have a Tale ready, it might be better to move the note to the front of the tale and replace it with a security clearance pulldown for an O5's-eye view of the object and phenomenon.

Also, I really liked the test logs in your sandbox, but you're right that they would have been a bit too much. Two thoughts on those:Will they be leaving camera packages/probes at the locations for later pick-up? Are the more fantastic locations being created whole-cloth, or being forced to conform by the SCP? I like the very, very SCP idea that the aliens that tended the agricultural fields were destroyed prior to "landing" because they were too inhuman or too "budget-intensive" to fit into they style of the original series.

I guess that's another way to win over some of the more dubious readers: put in enough reminders that this SCP object is referential to Trek and interacts with the tone and themes of Trek, but is still a SCP. The fact that half the researchers would lose objectivity instantly makes this thing more dangerous. The trips may be "fun" and "safe"(by SCP object standards), but at least some of the destinations are real, and might have later consequences (a la "Q Who" in TNG).

Footnotes
1. [Disclaimer: Advice from noob poster is taken at ones own risk. No assurance against the presence of invasive memes and infohazards is made.]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, no, I'm right there with you. If anything, it embodies the spirit of the 2000 contest itself, not the SCP Foundation Wiki as a whole.

I'm still drawing up a list of all kinds of crazy tests. I'll add DVD and book to the list. Check out Document 2000-LE-L on my Sandbox Page. I think you would like some of the stranger tests.

I'll have to think for a bit about what would happen with the DVD, but somehow I doubt it would work quite the same as with the Bookends.

I probably won't migrate the list of tests to the Mainlist until after the contest dust settles, since I'd need to rename it otherwise after I work out what my number is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oof! Hits me right in the fanboys!

This just flat out works for me, and I'm happy to see it looks to stick around. But I hope you'll forgive me for hoping it doesn't become the 2000. It's a great item; but not the sort of large, world-building piece I'd like to see in such a cornerstone slot.

I wonder why they haven't tried landing it on other objects. The self-referential craft might just be one of any number of destinations that would be interesting and relevant to the Foundation's charter. Land it on a dvd and see if it works like those bookends. Land it on a person and maybe you can visit their head-canon of the world. Heck, maybe land it on a sock and finally find out where the other half of the pair disappears to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Wondertainship, or the Reality Redefiner?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
requires Dr. Wondertainment'stm Epistemtastic Reality Redefinertm

please also make this I love it already

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah. Well, I intended it for paraphrasing. :B You know your meaning better than I!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno about "often die quickly." The tone is slightly better, but it changes the meaning of the sentence.

I've rewritten parts of that paragraph with an emphasis on tone. Let me know if you feel I've addressed the issue to your satisfaction.

Thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this SCP even though I never watched much Star Trek, but I have to agree with what people have said before about the red-shirt joke: "do not last long" doesn't really get the clinical tone that SCP tries for imho. Maybe change it to "often die quickly" or something similar? $.02

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Funny you should mention that. If they'd slapped me and told me "NO NAME BRANDS!" up front, Plan B was for a fins-and-chrome cigar-shaped spaceship by none other than the good Doctor himself. But as mentioned elsewhere, that would have been an entirely different skip with an entirely different feel, even if it performed exactly the same functions.

Hey Kidstm! Do you ever wonder what lifetm would be like… IN SPAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAACE!? Well, wonder no more. You would die instantly! But not with Dr. Wondertainmenttm's Intergalactic Exploration Settm! Now you and up to three friendstm can explore the mysteries of space, all from the comfort of your own bedroom!

  • Real Planets!
  • Imaginary Planets!
  • Impossible* Planets!

With the Dr. Wondertainmenttm Intergalactic Exploration Settm, now you too can explore the Universetm!

* requires Dr. Wondertainmenttm's Epistemtastic Reality Redefinertm, sold separately.

Oddly enough, this idea felt more derivative to me than the Star Trek tribute would have been. I'm not sure why. Maybe because there's plenty already like it on the site. Or maybe I was just afraid Dr. Wondertainment would take a dimmer view of Fair Use than Paramount. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, OS-9 knows just how to sell it, doesn't he? :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "EMMA! GO GET ME MY SPACE SUIT!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yeah, its the binary. heres a suggestion:

corrupt stream entered :ccorruup?? entttetereddd??? open

maybe randomize the ???'s. Im sure there's a script for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get it. Is 'fulenter' an author avatar or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…

Something just hit me like a sack of bricks. You know what that sounds like, don't you? To keep it contained, you need an imagination and a sense of joy. Whimsy. You need to be looking for something wonderful. Something that goes beyond just entertainment…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. But the way you phrased it, it could also be interpreted as "The reason you should bring along a D-class is because they might die." I know it's not what you were going for, but… it's exactly the type of ambiguous wording you don't want in a document like these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I like this. Is it wish fulfillment? Kind of, yes. But it's so charming, and has the spirit of optimism I think we could use more of on the mainlist. I'm not sure if it belongs in the 2000 slot, it somehow feels a little too slight for that, but it's a sweet and enjoyable article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I got out of it was that having one there is beneficial to the non-D personnel because the Ds will die first, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my head, it stood for "Landing Event - Log," but I see your point.

"bring along at least 1 D-class on Landing Events because they have a statistically higher tendency to die during them".

I… do not think that means what you think it means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You'd just need some sort of indicator of what, exactly, the 'test' is, and/or why it's necessary to do whatever it is they're doing.

As for the redshirts, all you really need is "bring along at least 1 D-class on Landing Events because they have a statistically higher tendency to die during them".

Oh, and I wanted to mention too that you might want to redo the designation of that one document, because "lel" is generally a corruption of "lol" and I don't think that's what you're going for. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, glad you liked it. :)

The redshirt joke's a work in progress apparently. I've already made it subtler a couple of times. I feel like it's down to the point where if I make the preparer of the document any less self-aware about it, it won't seem plausible that you could have a room full of PHDs discussing this thing and have it not come up. He doesn't WANT to say "this thing totally kills off the D-class first because they're like redshirts" in the report without more solid evidence, but they're all thinking it. Just like you were all thinking it.

I agree that it feels like a SCP prior to the note. Forum and IRC feedback was meh before I added the note, though. My goal when adding the note was to tie everything back to the central theme of the skip, which is pretty obviously an SCP tribute to Roddenberry's original series.

If you have any concrete suggestions on how to better couch the contents of the end note in the article, I would like to hear them. Clicking on a hidden tag is pretty much the only tool I am aware of for adding this type of interactivity to a wikidot document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best part is that you have to play with it and pretend to fly it around in order to keep it contained. That is adorable.

I for one like the redshirt joke, though you could certainly make it a good bit subtler. I also think it could work without the endnote. Not that the note is bad per se (I did have to read your explanation to get it), just that this has a really classic feel as an object and doesn't really need a 'zinger' ending. Nor is there really a lot of 'zing' in that note, since the 'aptitude test' consists, out of universe, of just clicking a link.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I've mentioned this before (though through the magic of wikidot, on it happened in the past on subsequent pages,) this was easily the most controversial element of the skip.

Actually, I may further tweak the wording here. It's just that given the Landing Event logs I've written up so far on the Sandbox page, they haven't had too many deaths. A partial solution may be to de-emphasize the Redshirt joke a little bit. But I will not remove it. Not while the comments saying "it's my favorite part" and "this ruined the skip for me" are still neck-and-neck.

My position is that if it's controversial, and it reminds people of Trek, I'd rather have it in the skip than not have it, on the grounds that removing stuff makes the skip blander. If everyone hated it, of course it would be gone as soon as I could edit the document.

Edit: Now that I think about it, that's terrible reason for either keeping or removing a line! I shouldn't be worried about what people think, I should just be focusing on whether or not it adds to the article. I think I just grew a little bit as an author.

I have removed the comical list of causes of death from the sentence, see if you like how it reads now any better. I really enjoyed the old version, but I feel like this version flows better and fits into the article better. It preserves Clinical Tone, while the old version was questionable at best.

Apologies to those of you who loved the Redshirts Joke in its original form. AND those of you who wanted me to remove it altogether. I've probably pissed off both camps with this compromise. X3 Oh, well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do you mean that using the binary was cliché, or that the ending exposed by clicking the binary was cliché?

I mean, either way, I pretty much agree with you. :) But in both cases, I had my reasons for invoking those tropes unironically.

I'm open to suggestions for a better collapsible, if it's the former.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am not a Trekkie (in fact I generally am iffy on Trek) but I really enjoyed this and found it charming. The end note almost went too far, but it still worked for me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i think the binary at the end was bad. A tad too cliché.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This response puts a big grin on my face. XD Glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AAAAHHHHH!!! AHAHAHAHAHAHAH!! AAAAAAAAAAAAHHHHHHH!
That was the first thing I noticed when they mentioned the battery cover… and how it was NEVER said it couldn't 'land' without it…
'what if you land it on the battery cover? Inception?'

Then you USED it! DAAAAAAMN!

Sorry, a bit too happy. Upvoting now. XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was the worst part. If it hadn't been there, I would've upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I couldn't NOT upvote this. While this isn't my favorite candidate for SCP-2000, this needs to be on the mainlist, and I can't wait to read your tales. Thank you for the great read! (I loved the redshirts.)
